contest.title: European Championship 2025 - Online Mirror (Unrated, ICPC Rules, Teams Preferred)

sources.notes: Statement and constraints: schedule each attraction exactly once; attraction i is available at positive multiples of a_i; t <= 2000, sum n <= 2000, a_i <= 1e9.
Official Codeforces tutorial page for the contest. Checked as requested; the static snapshot exposed the official K entry but not the expanded tutorial text.
Official EUC solutions PDF; used for the complete K solution: time-expanded flow, lazy multiple generation, and the O(n * E), E = O(n log n), bound.
Re-derived the minimum-time formulation as a bipartite b-matching between period groups and operation minutes, then matched it to the official incremental-flow algorithm.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate source requested by the user. It was attempted, but no usable solution text was exposed in the accessible snapshot, so it is not used as evidence.

annotation.summary: Given n ride periods a_i, assign every attraction to a distinct minute when it operates and minimize the last assigned minute.
annotation.constraints: 1 <= t <= 2000, 1 <= n <= 2000 per test, sum n over tests <= 2000, 1 <= a_i <= 1e9. The answer can be much larger than n, so enumerating all minutes up to the answer naively is too slow.
annotation.core_idea: Compress equal periods: period p_j has q_j identical attractions. For any set of minutes, feasibility is a max-flow or bipartite b-matching instance with S -> A_j capacity q_j, A_j -> B_i capacity 1 when p_j divides minute i, and B_i -> T capacity 1. The earliest finish is the first minute at which this flow can reach n. The optimized solution builds this graph lazily: maintain an ordered map where M(i) contains period indices whose next potentially useful multiple is i. Initially put j in M(p_j). Repeatedly take the smallest nonempty i, add minute vertex B_i and edges from all A_j in M(i), then try to augment the flow. If the flow increases, those periods may still need later multiples, so insert j into M(i + p_j); if it does not increase, the current cut shows this frontier contributes no useful future edge, so the chain is stopped. Since each processed time either increases the flow or removes at least one pending period index, only O(n log n) useful edges are generated by the official analysis.
annotation.complexity: O(n * E) time with incremental unit augmentations, where the official analysis gives E = O(n log n); O(E) memory.
tricks: compress equal a_i values into capacities
model ride scheduling as bipartite b-matching / max flow
generate only potentially useful multiples of each period
stop advancing a period chain when its current candidate minute cannot augment the flow

solution_variants.name: lazy-multiple-incremental-flow
solution_variants.summary: Maintain a max-flow network from period groups to generated minute vertices. Process generated minutes in increasing order and attempt one augmentation after adding the current minute. When an augmentation succeeds, schedule the next multiple for every period connected to that minute; otherwise discard those pending period advances. Stop when the total flow reaches n and output the last processed minute.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n * E), E = O(n log n), O(E) memory.

tags.evidence: The feasibility condition and the optimized algorithm are both expressed as a max-flow network with capacities from period groups to unit-capacity minute vertices.
The problem is exactly to schedule each attraction into one distinct available minute while minimizing the maximum scheduled time.
An attraction group of period p_j can be assigned only to minutes divisible by p_j, and the lazy graph generation advances through multiples i, i + p_j, ... .
Ignoring source and sink, the assignment graph is bipartite between compressed period nodes and minute nodes.
After expanding capacities, feasibility is equivalent to matching attractions to distinct feasible minutes; the implementation keeps the grouped-capacity form and solves it by flow.
The lazy algorithm needs an ordered map or min-priority queue over generated minute values to process the smallest pending multiple next.
